<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.7) -->

# Jobs Are Immutable: Use Sync Hooks for GitOps

Kubernetes `Job` objects have a largely immutable `spec.template`. When a
GitOps controller (Argo CD, Flux, plain `kubectl apply`) re-applies a Job
manifest whose template differs from the live object, the API server
rejects the patch — silently, from the controller's perspective. The
live Job keeps its old spec and the change in git never takes effect.

With `prune: false` (or any equivalent retention setting), the controller
will not even delete the stale Job. Manual `kubectl delete job` becomes
a prerequisite for every change.

## The Rule

Treat every Job manifest checked into a GitOps source as
**delete-and-recreate on every sync**, not patch-in-place.

### Argo CD

Annotate the Job as a Sync hook:

```yaml
metadata:
  annotations:
    argocd.argoproj.io/hook: Sync
    argocd.argoproj.io/hook-delete-policy: BeforeHookCreation
```

`hook: Sync` runs the Job as part of every sync (after the wave's normal
resources). `hook-delete-policy: BeforeHookCreation` deletes the prior
instance before creating the new one, sidestepping the immutability
rejection.

If the Job should run only once at install time (e.g. a bootstrap that
seeds initial data), use `PreSync` instead of `Sync` and decide whether
`BeforeHookCreation` or `HookSucceeded` is the right cleanup policy.

### Flux

Use the equivalent `kustomize.toolkit.fluxcd.io/force: "Enabled"` on the
Kustomization, or model the Job as a Helm chart resource and let
HelmRelease handle replacement. The general principle is the same: the
controller must delete the old Job before applying the new manifest.

### Plain `kubectl apply`

`kubectl replace --force` works for one-off operator action but is not a
GitOps strategy. If you find yourself running it routinely, move the Job
behind a controller that handles delete-and-recreate.

## Why this matters

- The failure is silent. The controller reports "Synced" because the
  apply call returned without error — the per-field rejection is buried.
- Stale Jobs hide real bugs. A "successful" Job from last week's manifest
  passes monitoring while the new logic in git never ran.
- The workaround (manual `kubectl delete job` before each change) is
  exactly the toil GitOps exists to eliminate.

## When to apply

- Any `Job` manifest stored in a GitOps source tree.
- Any one-shot work modeled as a Job (bootstrap, migration, smoke test,
  IAM provisioning).

## When skipping is acceptable

- Jobs created by a controller (CronJob, Argo Workflow, Tekton
  PipelineRun) — the controller generates unique names per run, so
  immutability does not apply.
- Single-tenant test clusters where manual `kubectl delete job` between
  applies is an acceptable cost.
